title: Lanka — the War of the Ramayana
subtitle: devotion, loyalty and dharma on the causeway to Lanka

raja.name: Rama
raja.moral: Dharma above all — even above the self
raja.teaching: Rama is maryada purushottama, who keeps his word though it costs him a kingdom. Guard him as you would guard your own integrity; when he falls, the cause is lost.

mantri.name: Vibhishana
mantri.teaching: Vibhishana ranges the whole field and dares to leave a wrong side for a right one. Wise counsel that serves dharma is stronger than any army.
mantri.fall: When the true counsellor is lost, kings march blindly into their own ruin.

ratha.name: Ratha
ratha.teaching: The chariot charges the straight road of duty and does not turn aside. Choose your line and press it with force.
ratha.fall: A shattered chariot warns that speed without protection ends in the dust.

gaja.name: Kumbha-Gaja
gaja.teaching: The elephant treads its diagonal with patient, crushing power. Great strength is a blessing only when it keeps to its dharma.
gaja.fall: The great beast falls, and the field learns that pride is heavier than tusks.

ashva.name: Hanuman-Ashva
ashva.teaching: Like Hanuman crossing the ocean, the Ashva leaps where no straight road exists. Faith and courage go where reason alone cannot.
ashva.fall: A fallen leaper reminds us that even the boldest heart is mortal — spend it wisely.

padati.name: Vanara
padati.moral: The strength of the humble many
padati.teaching: The vanaras built a bridge across the sea, stone by stone. The humblest soldier, never retreating, does the patient work that wins the war.
padati.fall: One vanara falls so the bridge may hold — no small sacrifice for dharma is ever wasted.

pawnTeachings: Stone by stone the vanaras bridged the sea — great works are built one small step at a time.
The monkey-warrior never retreats; keep your face toward your dharma.
Even the smallest servant of a great cause carries the whole cause within.
Devotion, not size, moved Hanuman across the ocean — carry your task with a full heart.
Hold the line beside your brothers; a bridge of many hands outlasts any lone hero.
The first stone is the hardest to lay; after it, the causeway is only duty.
You cannot un-lay a stone — weigh each step, then commit it fully.
A humble guard protects more than it strikes; steadfast presence is its own strength.
The sacrifice of the small opens the victory of the whole.
Serve without clinging to reward — build the bridge, and let the sea be crossed.
captureLines: Even in a righteous war, every fallen foe was once someone's kin — strike without hatred.
The victor bows to the fallen; dharma asks for justice, never cruelty.
A piece falls and the causeway advances — attachment is the only true loss.
Do your duty cleanly; the fallen too played their part in the turning of the tale.
Rama mourned even Ravana's rites — honour the enemy you must defeat.
promotionLine: The steadfast Vanara, having crossed the whole field, is raised to a counsellor's rank. Devotion carried to its end becomes wisdom.
checkLine: Rama is threatened — rally the Vanara Sena and shield the heart of the cause.
checkmateTitle: Jaya Shri Rama — Victory of Dharma
checkmateLine: Lanka falls and dharma is restored. Reflect: victory is hollow unless it serves what is right.

raja.name: Ravana
raja.moral: Power without restraint is the seed of ruin
raja.teaching: Ravana's ten heads held every art and scripture, yet a single ungoverned desire undid them all. Guard the centre of your being — great power without dharma destroys itself.
raja.fall: When Ravana falls, ten crowns roll in the dust — no learning saves the one who will not master himself.

mantri.name: Indrajit
mantri.moral: Genius bound to a wrong cause
mantri.teaching: Indrajit ranged the whole field and even conquered the heavens, yet his brilliance served adharma. The sharpest mind is wasted, even ruinous, when it defends what is wrong.
mantri.fall: When the great conqueror falls, Lanka learns that no weapon can defend an unjust cause forever.

ratha.name: Ratha
ratha.teaching: The demon-chariot charges without turning, but a straight road to a wrong goal only arrives at ruin the faster. Choose your direction before you build your speed.
ratha.fall: A shattered demon-chariot warns that force in a bad cause still ends in the dust.

gaja.name: Kumbhakarna
gaja.moral: Vast strength, spent in the wrong service
gaja.teaching: Kumbhakarna woke from his slumber to fight for a brother he knew to be wrong, yet fought all the same. Loyalty is noble, but strength owed to adharma becomes a tragedy.
gaja.fall: The titan falls, and the field learns that even mountains of might cannot hold up a falling cause.

ashva.name: Rakshasa-Vira
ashva.teaching: The demon-champion leaps where none expect, striking from the shadows. Cleverness is a blade that cuts both ways — unguided by conscience, it wounds its own wielder.
ashva.fall: A fallen demon-champion reminds us that trickery, unmoored from truth, is still mortal.

padati.name: Rakshasa
padati.moral: The courage of the misled many
padati.teaching: The rakshasa foot-soldier never retreats, marching bravely for Lanka. Courage is real even in a wrong cause — so choose a cause worthy of your courage.
padati.fall: A rakshasa falls for Lanka's shadow — no soldier's bravery is small, though the cause may be.

pawnTeachingsDark: The rakshasa never retreats — but bravery is only as good as the cause it serves.
Even a soldier of the wrong side fights with a full heart; give that heart to what is right.
One demon marches for Lanka's pride; ask always whose pride your steps defend.
Strength offered to ego builds a wall that will one day fall on its builder.
Hold the line for your brothers — yet a wall raised for adharma cannot stand.
The first step into a wrong war is the hardest to unmake; weigh it before you take it.
You cannot un-march a step — so be sure of the banner you follow.
A demon guards Lanka's gate, unaware the true enemy stands within its king.
The sacrifice of the misled is the heaviest loss — spend yourself for truth.
Serve, but never blindly — even a foot-soldier answers for the cause he carries.
captureLinesDark: The rakshasas strike hard, yet every blow for adharma recoils upon Lanka.
A foe falls to the demon host — but a victory without dharma is a debt, not a gain.
Lanka advances a square, and the shadow lengthens; attachment to conquest is its own defeat.
Strike for your king if you must, but know whose cause your sword truly serves.
Even Ravana's warriors were valiant — valour spent for ego is the saddest waste.
promotionLineDark: A rakshasa foot-soldier, having crossed the whole field, is raised to a captain of Lanka. Yet rank in a falling cause only means a longer fall.
checkLineDark: Ravana is threatened — the rakshasa host closes ranks to shield the demon-king of Lanka.
checkmateLineDark: Lanka's shadow triumphs for a moment. Yet reflect: a victory raised on adharma carries within it the seed of its own undoing.
